narrative: Your law school application was denied because our predictive model, which evaluates the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated a 41.3% chance that you would fail it—above the threshold we use for admissions decisions. This prediction draws from patterns in academic records, test scores, and background details of past students, focusing on factors that reliably signal bar exam success without considering gender or race. In your case, the strongest influence pushing toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 grade decile of 1, which placed you in the bottom tenth of your school that year—far below the typical top-half standing (around the 6th decile) seen among students who passed the bar. This early stumble in later undergraduate years stood out as the most significant concern, as it often correlates with challenges on the rigorous bar exam. On a more positive note, your high family income bracket worked strongly in your favor, aligning with a smaller but successful group of passers from similar backgrounds and pulling the prediction toward success. Your LSAT score of 33 also helped nudge things positively, though it fell short of the near-37 average among those who passed, providing some reassurance about your potential. Your undergraduate GPA of 3.20 was close to the 3.24 average for bar passers, and opting for full-time study matches the norm for 93% of successful students, but these played smaller roles overall. Compared to typical admitted applicants, who show stronger mid-to-late grade deciles and higher LSATs, your profile tipped just over the edge into higher risk. While this is disappointing, especially given your solid start in Year 1 and other strengths, it highlights where the model saw the biggest gap. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost it closer to 37 or above, and if possible, pursue postgraduate courses or experiences that could demonstrate improved performance in advanced work. Strengthening your later academic record through targeted prep or additional credentials might also help reframe your trajectory for future applications. Many students in similar positions have succeeded by addressing these key areas—reach out to admissions advisors for personalized strategies, and know this is just one data point, not your full story. With focused effort, you can shift the odds in your favor next time.
